turns.text: Hello Cristina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in chair, glancing toward the bedroom where the baby sleeps*

Yes, okay. Thank you for... for asking me. It's nice that someone wants to listen, actually.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

The pregnancy itself, it was... fine. I knew what to expect, I've done this before. But after, after the baby came... everything changed so fast. I thought I would be more prepared this time, you know? But it's different when you're alone. Very different.

*fidgets with coffee cup*

The biggest problem is just... getting places. I don't have a car right now, and this town, it's not easy without one. Three miles to the clinic feels like thirty when you have a newborn and no way to go. I called several times, but they say I need to come in, and I'm thinking, yes, I *want* to come in, but how? The bus schedule doesn't match his feeding times.

*voice becomes quieter*

And when I finally do get there, I think the doctors see me and they assume... I don't know. They speak slowly, use simple words. They don't know that I understand the science. I spent years studying diseases, infections, how they affect babies. But I can't explain this to them because my English gets worse when I'm stressed and tired. So they treat me like I don't understand anything.

*looks up*

It's isolating. That's the word, yes? I feel very alone in this.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

*leans back, rubbing temples*

Ah, well... I thought I knew what to expect. Four babies, you understand? I've been through this. I thought, okay, bleeding for a few weeks, some soreness, my body will feel tired but it will heal. The normal things.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

But this time, it's different. I think because I'm older — thirty-eight is not young anymore for having babies — and because I'm doing it completely alone. There's no one to help, no one to say "rest, I'll take the baby." So my body doesn't have time to recover properly.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The bleeding lasted longer than before. I was worried about it, but I couldn't get to the doctor to ask if it was normal. I just... waited. And the pain, especially when I'm tired, it comes back. I thought by now it would be gone, but some days I feel like I'm six weeks postpartum still, not... whatever week I'm actually in. I lost count.

*voice tightens*

The hardest part is that my body needs rest, but my mind won't let me rest. I'm listening for him all the time — is he breathing okay? Does he have a fever? — and I'm also working, trying to keep money coming in. So physically, I'm exhausted in a way I wasn't before. My recovery feels... stalled. Like I'm stuck in the middle of healing and can't quite finish it.

*pause*

I expected my body would bounce back. It hasn't.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

*sighs heavily, wrapping arms around herself*

Before, I told myself I would breastfeed. I did it with my other children, so I thought... it should be the same, no? Natural, you know? I didn't expect it to be easy exactly, but I thought my body would know what to do.

*voice becomes strained*

The reality... it was painful in ways I didn't remember. Or maybe I forgot. The engorgement was terrible — my breasts were so hard and swollen I could barely touch them. And he couldn't latch properly at first, so it just hurt and hurt. I cried. A lot.

*pauses, looking down*

And there was no one to help me. No nurse coming by to check, no mother or sister to sit with me and say "this is normal, it will get better." I found information online — old research papers, lactation studies — and it made me more anxious, not less. I could see all the ways it could go wrong.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I'm still breastfeeding, but some days I resent it. I resent that my body is the only source of food, that I cannot rest, that no one else can take this from me. And I feel guilty for resenting it. A good mother should want this, right?

*looks up with tired eyes*

The support I expected... it doesn't exist. No one called. No lactation consultant. Just me, alone with my breasts and my fear that I'm not doing it right.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

*leans forward, eyes suddenly focused and intense*

Yes. Yes, exactly. There is a big gap.

*counts on fingers*

They gave me a pamphlet. One paper with pictures showing how to hold the baby — like I haven't held three babies before. And a phone number for a hotline, but it's during business hours and I'm working then. When I have time at night, the line is closed.

*voice becomes more agitated*

What I needed was someone to come to my house. To see me actually trying to feed him, to watch and say "here, adjust your hand this way" or "your latch looks good, the pain will decrease." I needed someone to listen when I said I was struggling, not just give me a paper.

*pause, more thoughtfully*

And I needed them to ask about my situation. To understand that I'm alone, that I'm working full-time, that I don't have time to sit in a clinic waiting room for two hours to see someone for five minutes. They could have offered home visits. They could have called to check on me. Simple things.

*voice becomes smaller*

I understand the science of lactation — I've read the studies, the hormones, everything. But understanding it in a paper is not the same as living it when your nipples are bleeding and you haven't slept in thirty hours and there's no one to help you decide if you should keep going or switch to formula.

*looks directly at you*

The support they offered... it wasn't for someone like me. It was for someone with a husband, a mother nearby, time to make appointments.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

*goes very still, staring at hands*

I didn't expect to feel this way. I thought... I thought I would be happy. Tired, yes, but happy.

*voice wavers slightly*

Before the baby came, there was already so much pain. The divorce was finalized just before I got pregnant — it was complicated, messy. I thought having a new baby would be a fresh start, something hopeful. I thought maybe the sadness would fade into the background.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

Instead, it got worse. Much worse.

*looks away*

In the first weeks, I couldn't stop crying. Not just tired crying — deep, dark crying where I couldn't see why anything mattered. And I was terrified all the time. Every time he slept, I would check if he was breathing. Every little sound made my heart race because I know — I *know* — what infections can do to newborns. The clinical knowledge I have, it became a curse. I see danger everywhere.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I didn't tell anyone at the doctor's office. They asked me the standard questions on the form, and I said "fine, everything is fine." But I wasn't fine. I was alone in this house with a baby and with three other children who needed me, and the weight of it... 

*trails off*

I expected to feel joy. Instead, I felt trapped. Guilty for not feeling the joy. Anxious about my son's health. Angry at my ex-husband for not being here. Angry at myself for getting into this situation.

*meets your eyes briefly*

No one prepared me for how lonely it would be.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

*sits back slowly, eyes distant*

Before... I was Cristina the scientist. The woman in the laboratory with the research, the credentials, the understanding. That was who I was. That made me feel... important. Smart. Valuable.

*voice becomes strained*

I thought having a baby would just be something I do, alongside everything else. I thought I would still be that person — the professional, the expert. Maybe a mother too, but not... not *only* a mother.

*pause, looking down*

But that's not what happened. Now when I introduce myself, I don't say "I'm a researcher studying infectious diseases." I say "I'm a single mom of four." That becomes the only thing. The only thing that matters, the only thing people see.

*voice tightens with frustration*

And the irony is so painful. I have this knowledge, this education, and it means nothing here. Nothing. I can't use it. I sit at home unable to get to work regularly because of transportation. I struggle to explain my concerns to doctors because my English fails me when I'm stressed. My laboratory skills, my years of training — they're just... gone. Useless.

*looks up with wet eyes*

I feel like I've disappeared. There's just the mother now, the tired woman trying to keep everyone alive. The scientist, the professional — I don't know where she went. She's still in here somewhere, but she's been buried so deep under diapers and worry and exhaustion that I can't find her anymore.

*voice breaks slightly*

And I'm grieving that. I'm grieving who I was.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

Everything. Everything was harder than I expected.

*stands up and walks to the window, looking out*

The professional part... I thought I could keep working. I have a job, I go when I can, but it's not the same. Before, I was focused. I could lose myself in the research for hours. Now I'm always half-present, always listening for my phone in case something happens at home. My manager, she's patient, but I can see the frustration. I'm not the reliable person I was.

*turns back, voice becoming more emotional*

And the social part... I had friends before. Colleagues. People I would talk to about ideas, about life. Now I don't have time. I don't have energy. When I try to call someone, what do I say? I'm exhausted and alone and scared? That's not... that's heavy. People don't want to hear that. So I stopped calling. Now I'm invisible in a different way.

*sits back down heavily*

The personal part — who I am as a woman, not as a mother or a professional — that disappeared the fastest. I don't remember the last time I did something just for me. I don't recognize myself in the mirror anymore. Not just because I'm tired, but because the person looking back doesn't feel like me.

*voice becomes quieter, almost ashamed*

And the hardest part? I didn't expect to lose my confidence. I'm a scientist. I should understand my own body, my own mind. But I don't. I feel lost in all of it, and that... that breaks something in me.

*pauses*

I thought intelligence would protect me from this. It didn't.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

*lets out a bitter laugh*

What I expected? Honestly, after four babies, I knew what the standard was supposed to be. I expected... someone to actually check on me. To see how I was healing, to ask real questions about how I was feeling mentally, to make sure the baby was growing properly.

*voice becomes frustrated*

What actually happened? I had one appointment at six weeks. One. The doctor — I'd never seen her before — spent maybe ten minutes with us. She did a quick physical exam, asked if I was bleeding still, if I was in pain. I said no to everything because I was afraid if I told the truth, she would think I was complaining too much.

*stands and paces*

She never asked about my mental health. She never asked if I was coping. She looked at my baby for maybe thirty seconds, said "he looks healthy," and that was it. In and out. There was a form with questions about depression and anxiety, but I filled it out while sitting in the waiting room with three other children and a screaming infant. Of course I said I was fine.

*voice becomes sharper*

And after that? Nothing. No follow-up. No one calling to check. No resources given to me about postpartum support, about how to access help if I needed it. Nothing.

*sits back down, exhausted*

I expected continuity of care. Instead, I got a checkbox. She checked the box that said I was seen, and then I was forgotten. If I hadn't driven myself — borrowed a car from a friend that day — I wouldn't have gone at all. There was no accommodation for someone like me.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

*rubs face wearily*

I didn't really have a choice about returning. I needed the money. There's no one else to support us, so... I went back.

*voice becomes tight*

Practically, I thought it would be hard but manageable. I have experience juggling things, right? Four children already. But what I didn't expect was how much harder it would be without a partner. Before, even with my ex-husband, there was someone to share the load. Now there's just me.

*stands and walks to the baby's room, checks on him, comes back*

The childcare is a nightmare. I found someone to watch the baby during my shifts, but it costs almost as much as I make. Some weeks I'm actually losing money by working. It makes no sense, but what else can I do? I can't leave him alone.

*voice cracks*

Emotionally... I feel like I'm failing at everything. At work, I'm distracted, tired, not the person they hired. At home, I'm absent when I'm supposed to be present. And with the baby, I'm guilty every single day that I'm not with him. I pump at work — that's humiliating in a laboratory setting — and I rush home to feed him, but there's never enough time.

*sits heavily*

I expected to feel like a professional woman with a baby. Instead, I feel like a bad mother who also happens to have a job. Neither role gets my best self anymore. I'm just... scattered. Broken into pieces that don't fit together.

*pause*

And the transportation problem makes it even worse. Some days I can't get to work because I can't find a ride.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

*laughs without humor, shaking head*

Oh, where do I even start?

*counts on fingers, voice becoming more agitated*

The feeding... I'm still breastfeeding, so I have to pump at work. In the laboratory. Where I'm supposed to be professional, sterile, focused on my research. And there's nowhere private to do it. I use the bathroom sometimes, but that's not sanitary. Other times I pump in my car on my lunch break, and I'm sitting there feeling like... like an animal.

*voice hardens*

And the milk — I have to bring it home, keep it cold. It adds stress to every day. Is the cooler cold enough? Will it spoil? And then when I get home, I'm supposed to feed him immediately, but I'm still in my work clothes, my mind is still at the lab. The transition is impossible.

*stands, pacing*

Childcare is the worst part. I pay someone to watch him, but I don't have a car to drop him off and pick him up consistently. Some days I'm late getting him because the bus is late, and the woman who watches him gets angry. She needs reliable payment, reliable schedules. I can't give her either of those things.

*voice becomes strained*

And my own health? I'm not sleeping. I'm not eating properly. I get to work exhausted and leave more exhausted. I haven't had a physical since the six-week checkup. I don't have time to get sick, but my body is breaking down.

*sits down, defeated*

I thought I could manage this. I can't.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

*goes very quiet, jaw tightens*

There is no partner. That's... that's the whole problem, isn't it?

*pause, voice becomes controlled but sharp*

The baby's father and I, we're not together. We haven't been for a long time. So when I got pregnant, I knew I would be doing this alone. I accepted that. Or I thought I did.

*looks away*

But accepting it in theory is different from living it. I expected... I don't know what I expected. Maybe that he would help more than he does? He gives some money, not much. He sees the baby sometimes, but not regularly. And when he doesn't show up — which is often — I have to explain to the older children why their father isn't here again.

*voice becomes bitter*

The caregiving is all me. Every diaper, every feed, every sleepless night. There's no one to say "I'll take him tonight, you sleep." There's no one to hold the baby while I shower. No one to talk to about my fears, to say "is this normal?" There's just me, all the time.

*looks directly at you, eyes hard*

And emotionally? I'm angry. I'm angry that I'm thirty-eight years old doing this alone. I'm angry that I made choices that led me here. I'm angry at him for not being present, even though I knew he wouldn't be.

*voice softens, becomes sadder*

I think I got pregnant hoping somehow it would be different. That maybe having a baby would... I don't know. Make things better. Make me feel less alone. Instead, it made everything worse.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

*laughs bitterly, shaking head*

The biggest surprise? That there was no partnership at all. That I was completely delusional about it.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I think... I think deep down, I hoped that having the baby would change things. That he would see this child and suddenly want to be involved, want to be present. That becoming a father would make him step up. That's the surprise — how much I wanted that to be true, and how quickly I had to accept that it wasn't.

*rubs eyes*

He came to the hospital when the baby was born. For maybe two hours. And I remember thinking, "Okay, this is the moment. He's going to be different now." But he wasn't. He went back to his life like nothing happened. I called him a few weeks later when I was struggling, when I was so tired I couldn't think straight, and he said he was busy.

*voice cracks slightly*

The biggest surprise was realizing I was completely alone. Not just practically — like, no one to drive me places — but emotionally alone too. I thought having a child together would create some kind of bond, some sense of shared responsibility. Instead, it just highlighted how little we actually meant to each other.

*pause*

And the worst part? I'm not even angry anymore. I'm just... resigned. Which somehow feels worse than anger.

*looks down*

I was naive to think a baby would fix anything.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

*takes a long, shaky breath, eyes distant*

Before I got pregnant, I thought... I thought maternity care would be this supportive system. I thought there would be people checking on me, asking how I was doing, making sure everything was okay. I thought it would be thorough — physical health, mental health, the baby's health. All connected.

*voice becomes hollow*

I thought someone would see me as a whole person. Not just a pregnant woman, not just a body producing a baby. Someone would understand that I was a professional, a mother of three already, a woman going through a divorce, alone. Someone would ask, "What do you need? How can we help you succeed?"

*stands, walks to window again*

The most significant gap... it's the loneliness. That's what shocked me the most.

*turns back, voice intense*

I expected maternity care to be a bridge — something that would hold me up during this transition. Instead, it was like... it was a gate that closed right after he was born. The pregnancy got attention — appointments, ultrasounds, monitoring. But the moment he arrived, I was on my own.

*voice becomes bitter*

No one asked about my transportation problems. No one offered solutions. No one said, "You're struggling with language barriers, let's get you an interpreter." No one checked if I was depressed or anxious. No one connected me to resources for single mothers. They just... moved on to the next patient.

*sits down heavily*

I expected to feel supported. Instead, I felt abandoned. And the cruel part? I'm educated enough to know exactly what good care looks like. I can see all the ways they failed me. That makes it worse somehow.

*voice drops to barely a whisper*

I expected maternity care to keep me alive. It did that, technically. But it didn't help me live.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

*sits forward, hands clasped tightly, speaking with sudden clarity*

I would change it so that the system actually sees who people are. Not just their medical numbers.

*voice becomes stronger*

Right now, the system assumes everyone has the same resources, the same support, the same life. It's designed for women with partners, with cars, with flexible jobs, with family nearby. If you don't fit that picture, you fall through. Completely.

*stands, pacing with purpose*

What I would do is this: before you even give birth, the system needs to know your actual situation. Not just medical history — I mean, do you have transportation? Do you have childcare? Do you speak English? Are you alone? Are you safe at home? What are your real barriers?

*voice becomes more passionate*

And then — and this is important — the system needs to actually do something about it. Not hand you a pamphlet. Actual services. Home visits for women without transportation. Interpreters who understand not just language but culture. Mental health screening that's real, not a form you fill out in the waiting room. Flexible appointment times for women who work.

*sits back down, looking directly at you*

And the biggest thing? Continuity. One person who knows me, who follows me from pregnancy through the first year. Someone who asks, "How are you really doing?" and listens to the answer. Someone who connects me to resources before I fall apart.

*voice becomes quieter but firm*

The system should adapt to the woman. Not the other way around. Right now, I had to fit into their boxes or disappear. That's backwards.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, fingers tracing the rim of the cold coffee cup, eyes glistening*

There's one thing... something I haven't really said out loud.

*voice becomes very small*

I'm terrified that my knowledge is going to hurt my son instead of help him. That I know too much about what can go wrong — RSV, CMV, sepsis, all the things that kill babies — and I can't access the care to prevent it. I can't even get him to a checkup when I'm worried.

*looks up, voice shaking*

Last month he had a cough, and I knew exactly what it could become. I called the clinic three times. They said to bring him in, but I couldn't get a ride. I ended up driving him myself at midnight because I was so scared. And he was fine. He's fine. But what if next time I can't get there in time? What if my fear becomes a tragedy because the system failed us?

*wipes eyes*

And I want people to know that it's not just about being poor or being a single mother. It's about being invisible while drowning. I'm educated. I understand my own vulnerability. I can see exactly what's wrong with how I'm being treated. And that knowledge doesn't help — it just makes me more anxious because I can't do anything about it.

*voice becomes quieter*

Also... tell them that mothers need to be asked directly, "Are you okay?" Not on a form. In person. With someone who will actually listen and do something.

*pause*

That's all I have.
